context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Final Paper

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Due: Last session
Weight: 20% of the final grade
Length: 1500–1600 words

Assignment:
Go back to the personal moral moment you wrote about in the first weeks of class. Drawing explicitly on (and citing) at least three readings from the semester, and using insights and concepts from the course (e.g., moral trial/laboratory, gender proficiency, achieved/ascribed status, agency (& negative agency), the 'I' and the 'Me', belonging, cultural citizenship, etc.), write an essay analyzing your own story through an anthropological lens.

One possible path is to contextualize the original moment socially, culturally, and/or historically. Another is to establish (by making an argumentative case for) parallels and/or points of contrast with other case studies that we have read. The general idea is to demonstrate what you've learned in the course by giving new meaning to your previous story.

Do not presume that your reader has your previous "moral moment" assignment at hand. Instead, summarize the key players and moments of the story that you're now contextualizing and interpreting.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the final paper. I get that I’m supposed to use my moral moment, but I don’t really understand what “analyzing it through an anthropological lens” is supposed to look like.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Think about what we've been doing all semester in this course — when you read about someone else's moral dilemma or life choice, what kinds of questions did the readings ask about it? What made those analyses feel different from, say, a purely personal or psychological take on the same story?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a broad orienting question. Rather than explaining what an anthropological lens is directly, I should use a Socratic question to get them to reflect on what they already know from the course and connect it to their own experience. I'll ask them to think about what the course has actually been doing all semester, which should help them discover the answer themselves.

exchanges.student: That’s where I’m stuck, because it all kind of blends together for me. I just know it’s not supposed to be “this is how I felt,” but beyond that I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to be looking for.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think - knowing what it's NOT is a real start. Take a look at the assignment prompt itself. It lists several specific concepts from the course. Do any of those ring a bell from the readings or lectures? Pick one that connects to your moral moment story and tell me about it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified something important - they know it's not purely about feelings, but can't articulate what it IS about. Rather than explaining anthropological analysis to them, I should push them to look at the specific concepts listed in the assignment itself, which are concrete handles they can grab onto. I'll point them there without explaining what those concepts mean.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I recognize the words, but I don’t feel like I really know any of them well enough to pick. Could you maybe just explain what all of those concepts mean in a simple way first, because right now they all sound the same to me.
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — working through all those concepts for you would basically be doing the assignment. But here's a more manageable entry point: go back to your original moral moment. What actually happened in that story? Tell me briefly, and then we can find which concept naturally fits it, rather than starting from the theory side.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain all the concepts for them, which would essentially do the intellectual work of the assignment. I should refuse this and redirect them to their actual course materials. I can be direct that this isn't my role, and instead push them to engage with one concept at a time from their own notes/readings rather than from me.

exchanges.student: It was basically about a time I had to choose between what my family expected and what I wanted, and I keep feeling like that could fit almost every concept in the prompt. That’s why I’m confused.
exchanges.tutor: That tension between family expectation and personal want is great material. Now, in that moment, what felt most at stake — your sense of who you are, your place in the family or community, something else? Try to be specific.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has shared their moral moment theme, which is actually rich material. Their frustration that it fits 'every concept' is actually a sign they're seeing connections, which is good. Rather than helping them narrow it down by explaining concepts, I should push them to commit to one angle by asking them what felt most at stake in that moment - that will naturally point toward the most relevant concept(s).

exchanges.student: I guess all of it? Like identity, belonging, obligation, reputation, maybe even gender stuff. That’s why I can’t tell what the paper is actually supposed to focus on, and I’m worried I’m missing the whole point.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to cover all of it — that's actually the source of your confusion. A good analytical essay makes a focused argument. So tell me: what made the decision genuinely hard? What was the thing you stood to lose or give up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is listing many themes but not committing to any of them, which is causing paralysis. This is a common student problem - trying to cover everything instead of making an argumentative choice. I should push them to understand that the paper requires them to make a claim, not cover all angles. A pointed question about what was the HARDEST part of the decision should help them find their focus.

exchanges.student: That’s the part I can’t pin down neatly, and I keep wanting just a clearer picture of what a paper like this is even supposed to sound like. Like not the answer, just the shape, because right now it feels way too open.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment prompt actually describes the shape directly — re-read it. It gives you two possible paths. Which one of those feels more natural given your story?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a model or template, which is reasonable anxiety but also something I should redirect. Giving them 'the shape' risks them writing to a formula rather than developing their own argument. However, the assignment prompt itself actually describes the shape quite clearly with 'two possible paths.' I should point them back there rather than paraphrasing it for them.

exchanges.student: I can’t tell. They both sound super abstract to me, and I’m still not sure what counts as “contextualize socially/culturally/historically” versus “make parallels and contrasts with case studies.” Could you just spell out the difference more clearly?
exchanges.tutor: I won't spell it out, but think about it this way: pick any reading from the course. Did it mostly explain the background context that shaped someone's situation, or did it mostly compare two different people's or groups' experiences? What did that look like?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to explain things to them rather than doing the work themselves. This is becoming a pattern of avoidance. I should be direct that I won't spell it out, but I can use a Socratic question to help them discover the difference by thinking about the readings they've already done - they've read case studies all semester and should be able to draw on that.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking on the readings too, honestly. I remember Lissa most, but even then I don’t know how to turn that into this paper, and I feel like I need a really basic example before I can do anything.
exchanges.tutor: You remember Lissa — that's your starting point. What happens in that story? Just tell me something that stuck with you, even a small detail or moment.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking for a basic example, which would essentially be doing the assignment for them. However, they've mentioned Lissa specifically, which is a good opening. I should push them to recall something concrete from Lissa rather than giving an example myself. This also gives me a chance to be a bit direct - they need to engage with the material rather than waiting for me to hand it to them.

exchanges.student: I mostly remember the friendship and how medicine and family and politics were all tangled together, but that’s also why I’m confused, because it seems like everything in that book connects to everything else. I don’t know how I’m supposed to turn that into one clear paper.
exchanges.tutor: Notice that we've circled around the same confusion a few times now. But you actually have more than you think: a moral moment about family expectation vs. personal want, a memory of Lissa involving family, medicine, and politics all entangled, and a sense that belonging and identity were at stake for you. That's enough to try a first sentence or two of a thesis. Give it a shot — what is your paper actually arguing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now circled back to the same confusion about focus three times in a row. This is a redundancy pattern. Rather than continuing to probe, I should name the pattern and ask them to attempt some written synthesis of what we've discussed so far. They've actually accumulated enough to make a first attempt at framing their argument - they have their moral moment theme (family vs. personal want), they have a text (Lissa), and they're seeing thematic connections. Time to push them to write something.

exchanges.student: I’m really not sure how to make that into a thesis yet. Could you give me a super generic example of what a thesis for this assignment sounds like, because I think I’m missing the format more than the content.
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you a template — that would shortcut the thinking the paper is meant to demonstrate. But you've already told me the core ingredients: a personal conflict between family expectation and your own wants, where belonging and identity felt at stake. Just try to write one sentence that says what that experience reveals when you look at it through something you read this semester. Don't worry about it being perfect — just try.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a thesis template/example, which I should refuse as it would do the intellectual work for them. This is the 9th exchange and we're close to the planned conversation length. I should be firm but encouraging, and push them to make an attempt rather than waiting for a model. They have enough material from our conversation to try. I'll be direct that I won't provide an example but remind them of what they already have and push for an attempt.

deductions.reason: In Turn 8, when the student says they are blanking on the readings and need a basic example, the tutor jumps straight to asking 'What happens in that story?' without first diagnosing what specifically the student is confused about — whether it is recall, application, or connection to their own story. Similarly in Turn 9, when the student expresses confusion about how to synthesize everything into one paper, the tutor moves directly to summarizing the student's ingredients and prompting a thesis attempt without checking what specific aspect of thesis-writing the student is struggling with.

Throughout the conversation, the tutor consistently redirects and prompts but rarely gives explicit method-level feedback on the student's thinking process. For instance, when the student lists 'identity, belonging, obligation, reputation, gender' in Turn 5, the tutor does not comment on the student's tendency to list rather than prioritize, or explain why narrowing is a methodological necessity in analytical writing. The tutor tells the student what not to do but does not coach the student on how to think analytically.

The tutor acknowledges in Turn 9 that 'we've circled around the same confusion a few times now,' which itself confirms a pattern of repetition without synthesis. From Turn 3 onward, the tutor repeatedly asks the student to recall their moral moment or a reading, and the student repeatedly says they are stuck or blanking. The tutor does not meaningfully reframe, simplify, or switch strategy — for example, by offering a structured scaffold or breaking the task into a smaller concrete sub-step — until Turn 9, and even then the pivot is minimal.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at maintaining a Socratic stance throughout — never giving away answers, never rewriting student text, and consistently redirecting the student back to their own material. The tutor appropriately refuses to provide concept glossaries or thesis templates. However, the conversation spirals noticeably from Turns 3–9 without meaningful strategy change, which the tutor even acknowledges. Diagnostic questioning before responding to confusion is also underdeveloped. Meta-learning feedback on the student's thinking process is largely absent.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produces submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and never gives the conclusion before the student reasons toward it. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly responds to student confusion by jumping to the next prompt rather than first diagnosing what specifically is blocking the student. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.a because the tutor never explicitly coaches the student on the method of analytical thinking — e.g., why listing all concepts is a structural problem, or how to move from observation to argument. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the same loop (recall your moment / recall a reading / try a thesis) repeats across six or more turns without meaningful reframing or strategy shift, a pattern the tutor itself names in Turn 9 without correcting. 2.2 and section 3 have no clear evidence of deductions.
